commonIssues: Frost-tender: plant out only after all danger of frost has passed; killed by first hard frost outside zones 10-11
Not drought-tolerant — wilts quickly in dry soil, especially in containers
Avoid excessive irrigation and poorly drained soil: stem and root rots, damping-off of seedlings
Powdery mildew and Botrytis blight in humid, crowded plantings
Aphids, spider mites, and whiteflies are the most common pests
Many bedding cultivars produce little nectar and are poor pollinator resources despite attracting hummingbirds/butterflies
Appreciates light afternoon shade in very hot summer climates

source.citation: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Salvia splendens (Scarlet Sage): full sun (6+ hrs) to partial shade (2-6 hrs); not drought-tolerant; well-drained loam high in organic matter; hardy USDA 10a-11b, grown as annual elsewhere; suited to containers; aphids, spider mites, whiteflies, powdery mildew, botrytis, southern blight.
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Salvia splendens: 'It is not drought-tolerant, but it needs warmth'; regular watering, consistently moist, well-drained soil.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Salvia splendens: medium water; average, evenly moist, well-drained soils in full sun to part shade; winter hardy USDA zones 10-11, grown as warm-weather annual; set out after last frost date; 1-2 ft tall, compact cultivars 8-15 in for edging and containers.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Salvia splendens: hardy zones 10-11 only, treated as annual in St. Louis; start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, plant out after last frost (basis for frostSensitive, coldHardyMinC ~0 C, and spring planting windows).

Old Farmer's Almanac — Salvia growing guide: full sun 6-8 hours/day; water during summer if rainfall is less than 1 inch (25 mm) per week; salvia does not like excessive summer irrigation; garden plants need little feeding, feed container plants in spring; mix in compost at planting.
Old Farmer's Almanac — Salvia growing guide: 6-8 hours of sun per day and well-draining soil (basis for targetSunHours).

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Salvia factsheet: plant in spring after danger of frost is passed; apply a slow-release fertilizer formulated for annuals at planting; ideal in full sun, tolerates partial shade; problems include damping-off, stem/root rots, powdery mildew, Botrytis, aphids, spider mites, whiteflies.
NC State Extension Plant Toolbox — bloom 'summer to frost' (basis for productiveMonths June-October).

Container minimum derived from extension size data (NC State/MOBOT: 1-2 ft tall, 8-18 in spread, compact cultivars recommended for containers): a 20-25 cm (8-10 in) pot of ~7.5-10 L per plant is a conservative working minimum; extension sources confirm container suitability but publish no exact pot volume.

Confidence note: containerProfile liters, feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays (monthly container feeding inferred from Clemson slow-release-at-planting + Almanac spring container feeding), watering baseIntervalDays (3 days derived from ~1 inch/week guidance plus 'not drought-tolerant' container context), and heatStressC (35 C estimate; sources say 'heat-tolerant' without a threshold) are conservative derivations, not directly published numbers.
